title: Add coupon discount support to charge endpoint
summary: The panel converged hard on a real correctness bug and a real security bug, plus a lot of naming noise. Survivors: the discount_amount-always-zero math bug (c0, three personas agreeing), the silently-swallowed coupon catch block (c13, all four lenses), the PCI-DSS debug log of the full charge body (c29, agreement across security/perf/standards), the unknown-coupon console.log (c24), missing unit tests (c9), couponCode bypassing schema validation (c27), fixed-discount going negative (c28), the snake_case response drift (c25), commented-out FREESHIP (c7), the over-engineered strategy/factory (c1), and the `any` response cast (c26). I merged the three duplicate zero-discount clusters, the four duplicate empty-catch clusters, the four duplicate body-logging clusters, the two unknown-coupon-log clusters, the nine scattered naming nits into the single naming finding, the abstract-class-vs-interface point into the over-engineering finding, and the two `any`-cast clusters. I dropped c30: returning the full transaction object is pre-existing behavior (the original code already did res.json(transaction)) and the 'likely contains card data' claim is speculative, not introduced by this diff.

findings.title: discount_amount will always be zero
findings.message: Line 24 (`payload.amount = finalAmount;`) overwrites the original amount before line 35 calculates the discount: `response.discount_amount = payload.amount - finalAmount`. This will always be zero. Save the original before applying the coupon: `const originalAmount = payload.amount;` before the try block, then use `originalAmount - finalAmount` in the response.

findings.title: Over-engineered: strategy pattern + factory for six static coupons
findings.message: This uses an AbstractCouponStrategy with two concrete implementations and a factory to manage exactly six hardcoded coupon codes. This is YAGNI: the pattern adds 65 lines of polymorphic boilerplate for data that's really a lookup table. Replace with a simple `Record<string, { type: 'percent' | 'fixed'; value: number }>` mapping coupon codes, and a single `applyCoupon(amount, code)` function with straightforward if/else logic. The data becomes visible at a glance instead of buried in if-chains.

findings.title: Commented-out code instead of deleted
findings.message: The FREESHIP coupon is commented out instead of deleted. Commented code rots: readers won't know if it's a plan, rollback, or fossil. Git history preserves it; delete it now. Add a feature flag or config entry if a future 'free shipping' coupon is planned.

findings.title: Poor variable naming throughout the module
findings.message: Multiple cryptic or informal names: `doStuff` (→ `applyCoupon`), `tmp` (→ `strategy`), `x` and `p` (→ `discountedAmount`, `discountPercent`), `result2` (→ `finalAmount`), `amt` (→ `discountAmount`), `theData` (→ `requestBody`). Clear names prevent readers from decoding intent.

findings.title: New coupon math logic has zero unit tests
findings.message: The entire couponDiscount module is shipping without a single test. This is critical: discount math, percentage calculations, fixed-amount deductions, and invalid-coupon handling all lack coverage. The PR description acknowledges this (unchecked checkbox: "Add unit tests for the coupon math (TODO follow-up)"). Money-handling logic that ships untested is not acceptable. Add unit tests for: valid percentage coupons, valid fixed-amount coupons, invalid codes, edge cases (0 amount, discount ≥ amount), and that negative final amounts are caught or rejected.

findings.title: Silently swallowed error in coupon application
findings.message: The catch block (line 19–21) has only a TODO comment and silences all errors from `doStuff()`. If coupon application fails—invalid code, misconfiguration, or runtime error—the error is dropped, the original amount is charged, and the client gets a 201 with `coupon_code` set but `discount_amount = 0`. This lies: the response claims the code was used when it wasn't. Errors must be logged and either propagated or handled explicitly. Handle meaningfully or re-throw; never silence.

findings.title: Debug logging should use the project's logger, not console.log
findings.message: Line 58 uses `console.log('DEBUG: unknown coupon code:', couponCode)`. While this particular log is not sensitive, the project supplies a structured logger for a reason—to ensure all output is consistent, filterable, and redactable. Use `logger.debug({ couponCode }, 'unknown coupon code')` instead, or remove the debug log if it's not needed in production.

findings.title: Naming convention drift: snake_case in response
findings.message: Lines 31–33 add `discount_amount` and `coupon_code` to the response — snake_case — but REST APIs in this codebase use camelCase (e.g., `customerId`, `createdAt`). Use `discountAmount` and `couponCode` for consistency. Callers expecting camelCase will fumble on the inconsistency.

findings.title: Response object typed as `any`
findings.message: Line 31 uses `const response: any = transaction;` — lose all type safety. The response object should be properly typed so TypeScript can catch shape mismatches. Define an interface like `ChargeResponse { ...transaction fields... discount_amount: number; coupon_code?: string; }` and use it.

findings.title: couponCode extracted without schema validation
findings.message: Line 12 extracts `couponCode` directly from `req.body` before schema validation. If the schema doesn't include `couponCode`, it's never validated (no length, no format, no enum). Extract it after validation, or add it to the `chargeSchema` as an optional string with constraints: `couponCode: z.string().min(1).max(32).optional()`. Weak validation at the boundary lets bad data reach business logic.

findings.title: No validation that fixed discount doesn't exceed charge amount
findings.message: The `FixedCouponStrategy.apply()` at line 25–29 subtracts a fixed amount from the charge with no check that the result stays non-negative. If a $5 charge meets a $10-off coupon, the result is -500 cents — invalid. Add a guard: `return Math.max(0, amount - this.amount);` or throw an error so invalid coupons are caught, not silently turned into refunds.

findings.title: Unredacted payment data logged to console (PCI-DSS violation)
findings.message: Line 14 logs the entire request body via `console.log('DEBUG charge body:', JSON.stringify(theData))`. For a charge endpoint, this body contains card numbers, CVC, customer details, and amounts—all of which must never appear in plaintext logs. PCI-DSS explicitly forbids this. The project supplies a pino logger with redaction rules for exactly this scenario; using `console.log` defeats the control. Remove the debug log or replace it with `logger.debug({ amount: payload.amount, customerId: payload.customerId }, 'charge request')` to log only safe fields.
